core.claim: Drone corridor regulations create a patchwork of 50 state laws that paralyze interstate commerce
core.reasoning: Amazon, UPS, Wing have all cited regulatory fragmentation as the primary barrier to drone delivery deployment
core.impact: 800,000 drone delivery jobs and $15B in GDP remain unrealized due to regulatory patchwork

core.claim: Delayed drone delivery costs rural healthcare access—medication and organ transport bottlenecked
core.reasoning: Rural hospitals use drones for lab samples, blood, and medication; 34 states prohibit autonomous BVLOS, blocking medical drone networks
core.impact: 4,200 preventable deaths annually in rural areas where drone medical logistics could have delivered critical care supplies
core.evidenceSource: JAMA Rural Health Drone Medical Delivery Study 2023

core.claim: Federal preemption via FAA authority creates a single national framework enabling drone infrastructure buildout
core.reasoning: FAA Reauthorization Act 2023 already grants FAA authority; AFF's plan operationalizes it with mandatory BVLOS standards
core.impact: Federal framework enables the 4 major carriers to deploy within 24 months; rural coverage reaches 90% within 5 years
core.evidenceSource: FAA BVLOS Rulemaking 2023 / Industry Deployment Projections

core.claim: FAA preemption is a deregulatory action—it removes state authority, which is not 'substantially increasing' federal regulation
core.reasoning: Topicality violation: AFF's plan removes regulatory layers rather than adding new ones. Preemption displaces 50 state regulatory regimes—net regulatory activity decreases.
core.impact: T is a voting issue: limits, ground, and predictability all favor NEG's interpretation. AFF's definition makes every deregulatory action topical.
core.evidenceSource: Administrative Law Review, Federal Preemption Doctrine 2022

core.claim: FAA has already begun rulemaking on BVLOS—AFF's plan is happening in the status quo
core.reasoning: FAA published BVLOS NPRM in 2023; final rule expected 2025; status quo solves without AFF
core.impact: No inherency: the regulatory gap is being filled without a plan mandate

core.claim: Drone collisions with manned aircraft pose a catastrophic safety risk that outweighs delivery benefits
core.reasoning: FAA Near-Miss Database: 87 drone-aircraft near-misses in 2023; one collision at altitude could kill 200+ passengers
core.impact: Rush to deploy drones without solved collision-avoidance risks a mass-casualty aviation accident

core.claim: State frameworks are not the real barrier—airspace congestion management is unsolved at the federal level
core.reasoning: Low-altitude airspace (50-400ft) lacks UTM (UAS Traffic Management) infrastructure; preempting states doesn't build UTM
core.impact: AFF solves the wrong problem; real solvency requires UTM investment, not just preemption

core.claim: NEG's aviation safety risk is already managed by AFF's plan—mandatory detect-and-avoid systems
core.reasoning: AFF's BVLOS standards require FAA-certified detect-and-avoid hardware; collision risk drops to <0.001% per flight hour (equivalent to commercial aviation)
core.impact: Safety concern is a plan-included answer; drone safety standards are part of AFF's reform

core.claim: FAA NPRM is years from implementation—AFF's plan accelerates by mandating timeline
core.reasoning: NPRMs take 3-7 years to finalize; rural patients are dying now; AFF compresses to 18-month implementation
core.impact: Status quo NPRM process is too slow; urgency justifies the plan mandate

core.claim: AFF's plan includes UTM buildout funding—$500M in mandatory FAA infrastructure spending
core.reasoning: Plan text explicitly includes UTM as a co-requirement; NEG strawmanned a preemption-only version
core.impact: AFF solves both the regulatory and infrastructure barriers; UTM is plan-included
core.evidenceSource: Plan Advocacy / NASA UTM Cost Estimates

core.claim: 2AC dropped T entirely—automatic voting issue. No response = concession under policy debate norms.
core.reasoning: Dropped T shell: AFF had 480 seconds and spent zero of them on T. This is a concession in policy debate.
core.impact: T is a voting issue and AFF has conceded it. Round over on T.
core.evidenceSource: Policy Debate Norms / Merriam Drop Rule

core.claim: Detect-and-avoid doesn't solve the flocking problem—mass simultaneous drone launches overwhelm any system
core.reasoning: Amazon Prime Air projects 500M packages by 2030 = millions of daily flights in urban airspace; no UTM handles this density
core.impact: AFF's detect-and-avoid is designed for sparse traffic; the solvency vision creates the very congestion that crashes it

core.claim: T is a clean voting issue—AFF never answered. Extend the shell. Round ends here.
core.reasoning: Preemption = deregulation = non-topical by any reasonable interpretation. AFF's silence is damning.
core.impact: T: extend neg-t-1 and neg-t-ext. AFF gets no new 2AR arguments—they never engaged T.

core.claim: Plan adds new federal mandates—preemption is a net increase in federal regulatory obligations
core.reasoning: Federal preemption imposes standardized compliance requirements on all drone operators nationwide; states previously had no obligation to follow any uniform standard
core.impact: Net federal regulatory activity increases under AFF—plan satisfies the resolution

core.claim: MIT density study assumes unmanaged skies—AFF's UTM directly addresses congestion management
core.reasoning: UTM allocates flight corridors; density is managed by the system, not individual detect-and-avoid
core.impact: NEG's density impact is a plan non-unique: only relevant absent UTM, which AFF provides

core.claim: 1AR T answer is too little too late—2AC drop is the concession. 1AR is not 2AC.
core.reasoning: Policy debate norm: drops in the relevant speech are concessions. AFF dropped T in 2AC—the only speech that matters for answering a 1NC shell.
core.impact: T voting issue stands: AFF's late answer in 1AR doesn't resurrect a dropped shell.

core.claim: Even if T is a voting issue, judge should use substance to inform T decision—AFF has stronger ground
core.reasoning: 4,200 rural deaths vs. aviation risk: magnitude favors AFF; use this in T analysis
core.impact: Substantive record shows AFF's position is correct even if T is close

questions.admissionNote: AFF conceded UTM infrastructure is not currently funded
NEG confirmed T interpretation: any net deregulation is non-topical
AFF conceded 18-month timeline assumes no litigation delays
NEG confirmed they are voting-issuing on T drop in 2AC
